content.text: Large language models acquire general knowledge, grammar, reasoning patterns, and world representation during a compute-intensive phase called pretraining, which processes trillions of tokens of unlabeled text using self-supervised objectives. The resulting

is the capability ceiling for every downstream system—instruction-tuned assistants, RLHF-aligned chat models, and agentic pipelines all inherit their fundamental strengths and weaknesses from the pretraining run that produced them.

Pretraining is the first and most expensive stage of training a large language model. The model—a deep transformer—is exposed to vast text corpora and trained to minimize a prediction loss over token sequences. No human labels are required; the supervision signal comes directly from the data itself. After pretraining completes, the resulting checkpoint is called a
and can be subsequently fine-tuned for specific behaviors.
Three major self-supervised objectives exist in practice, each creating a different type of foundation model:

— predict the next token given all preceding tokens. This is a strictly left-to-right (autoregressive) objective. GPT-2, GPT-3, LLaMA, Mistral, Falcon, and almost every chat or agent backbone use CLM. The model learns to generate fluent continuations, making it naturally suited to open-ended text generation.

— randomly replace 15 % of tokens with a [MASK] sentinel and predict the masked tokens from bidirectional context. BERT and RoBERTa use this. MLM produces strong representations for classification and extraction but is not autoregressive; a separate decoding head or a seq2seq wrapper is needed for generation.

— replace contiguous spans of tokens with a single sentinel token and predict the missing spans. T5 uses this encoder–decoder variant. The encoder sees the corrupted text; the decoder autoregressively reconstructs the masked spans.

— auxiliary heads predict 2–4 future tokens in parallel. Some recent models add this as an optional auxiliary loss alongside CLM to improve data efficiency and representations without changing the primary generation interface.

For agentic AI purposes, CLM is almost universally the relevant objective, because all popular agent-facing LLMs—GPT-4, Claude 3, Llama 3, Mistral, Qwen, Gemma—are CLM-pretrained decoder-only transformers.

Agent systems are built on top of pretrained base models, not trained end-to-end from random weights. The practical implication is that every capability your agent will ever have—whether it understands JSON, reasons through multi-step problems, knows Python idioms, or handles scientific terminology—derives from what was present in the pretraining corpus and how well the model captured it. Instruction tuning, RLHF, and prompt engineering can surface and redirect capabilities; they cannot

Understanding pretraining also clarifies several recurring confusions in agent engineering. A base model will confidently continue any prompt in a chat-like direction but does not inherently follow instructions or refuse dangerous requests—those behaviors come from later alignment stages. A model with a knowledge cutoff of January 2024 cannot know about events after that date regardless of how it is prompted. A model pretrained mostly on English will perform worse on low-resource languages. All of these are pretraining artifacts, not bugs in the fine-tuning or deployment stack.

For teams evaluating or selecting a backbone model for an agentic product, pretraining choices encode: vocabulary size and tokenizer efficiency, context length capability, code quality (driven by the fraction of code in the corpus), multilingual coverage, and the presence or absence of factual knowledge in specific domains. These cannot be changed post-hoc without retraining.
Provides general-purpose knowledge and language understanding that would be impossible to encode manually.
Self-supervised—no annotation budget. Trillions of tokens of supervision come cheaply from web and book corpora.
A single pretrained checkpoint can be fine-tuned for many downstream tasks at far lower cost than training each from scratch.
Code-heavy pretraining data instills structured reasoning and knowledge of APIs, improving tool-use and JSON-format reliability.
Long-context pretraining (4 k–128 k tokens) gives the model the ability to process extended documents, conversation histories, and tool call logs that are essential in agentic loops.
Emergent capabilities—complex reasoning, chain-of-thought, in-context learning—appear at scale without being explicitly trained, giving agents abilities beyond what their fine-tuning data covered.

Pretraining a state-of-the-art model from scratch requires tens of millions of dollars in compute. Very few organizations can afford to run a full pretraining run; most build on existing open or proprietary base checkpoints.
The model is frozen to the world as of its training cutoff. Factual knowledge, recent events, and new software versions are absent unless retrieved externally.
Data quality in the web corpus introduces biases, factual errors, toxic content, and copyright-questionable text. These cannot be completely filtered out and influence model behavior.

Longer pretraining context windows impose quadratic memory cost in vanilla attention; mitigations (flash attention, grouped-query attention, sliding windows) help but still constrain architectural choices.
Emergent capabilities appear unpredictably at scale, making capability forecasting difficult and occasionally surprising safety researchers.

CLM-pretrained models have only left-to-right attention during pretraining but are sometimes used in tasks that benefit from bidirectional context; this mismatch is partially mitigated by instruction tuning.

The pretraining process can be broken into four tightly coupled components: the objective function, the data pipeline, the model architecture, and the distributed training infrastructure. Each component affects the quality of the resulting base model.
, CLM minimizes the average negative log-likelihood of each token given its left context:
is computed by passing the hidden state at position
through a linear layer (the language model head) to obtain logits over the vocabulary of size
is the final transformer hidden state at position
. Because the loss is summed over every position, every forward pass provides
supervision signals, which is why next-token prediction scales so well.
For MLM, a random 15 % of input tokens are replaced by a [MASK] token and the loss is computed only on those positions:
is the set of masked positions and
is the corrupted input. Because the transformer can see future tokens, MLM produces richer bidirectional representations but cannot generate autoregressively without modification.

Raw web data from crawls such as CommonCrawl, C4, or RedPajama undergoes a multi-stage cleaning pipeline before tokenization. Quality filtering removes documents below a minimum word count, with high symbol density, or with a low ratio of alphabetic characters. Exact or near-duplicate documents are removed using hash-based deduplication (SHA-256 or MinHash-LSH). Toxicity classifiers and PII detectors make additional passes. The cleaned text is then tokenized—typically with Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) or SentencePiece—and packed into fixed-length sequences to eliminate padding waste. Domain mixing ratios determine what fraction of each batch comes from web text, books, code, scientific papers, and multilingual sources.
Models with billions of parameters do not fit on a single GPU. Modern pretraining combines several parallelism strategies:
— each GPU holds a full model copy and processes a different batch shard. Gradients are synchronized via AllReduce after each backward pass.
— individual weight matrices are split across GPUs so no single device holds the full matrix.
— model layers are partitioned across GPUs; micro-batches flow through the pipeline with overlapping compute and communication.
— parameters stored in BF16 or FP16 with FP32 gradient accumulation reduces VRAM by ~2× and increases throughput.
— intermediate activations are discarded during the forward pass and recomputed during backprop, trading 30 % extra compute for dramatically lower VRAM.
Virtually all large-scale pretraining runs use a schedule with a linear warmup phase followed by cosine annealing:

attrs.latex: \eta_t = \begin{cases} \eta_{\max} \cdot \dfrac{t}{t_{\text{warm}}} & t \le t_{\text{warm}} \\ \eta_{\min} + \tfrac{1}{2}(\eta_{\max} - \eta_{\min})\left(1 + \cos\dfrac{\pi(t - t_{\text{warm}})}{T - t_{\text{warm}}}\right) & t > t_{\text{warm}} \end{cases}

content.text: , warmup over the first 0.1–1 % of steps. The AdamW optimizer with
is standard. Gradient clipping at norm 1.0 prevents divergence from outlier batches.
The diagram below shows the five-stage LLM pretraining pipeline from raw data collection through the base model checkpoint.

Pretraining from scratch is the right choice when no existing public or licensed checkpoint covers the required domain, language, or architecture constraints—and when the organization has the budget (millions of dollars in compute) and data (trillions of tokens) to justify it.
In practice, most teams do not run a full pretraining. Instead they choose between:
— the default for virtually every agent product. Download a Llama 3, Mistral, or Falcon checkpoint and fine-tune it.

— resume training from an existing checkpoint on domain-specific data. Use this when the base model lacks coverage of specialized vocabulary or knowledge (medical, legal, scientific, programming in a niche language).
— train from random initialization. Justified for proprietary data that cannot be exposed to third-party base models, extreme-scale research runs, or unique architectural experiments.

Continued pretraining is far more accessible. A 7 B model can be continued-pretrained on tens of billions of domain tokens in days on a single 8-GPU node, whereas full pretraining of a 7 B model from scratch requires thousands of GPU-days.

The following example demonstrates a minimal but structurally complete causal LM pretraining loop in PyTorch. It implements the causal self-attention mask, warmup schedule, gradient clipping, and the cross-entropy CLM loss. This is educational; production runs use distributed training frameworks (see Tooling section).

# Minimal causal-LM pretraining loop (educational, PyTorch)
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
from torch.optim import AdamW
from torch.optim.lr_scheduler import CosineAnnealingLR

# ----- Hyperparameters -----
VOCAB_SIZE   = 32_000
SEQ_LEN      = 1024
BATCH_SIZE   = 8
HIDDEN_DIM   = 512
N_HEADS      = 8
N_LAYERS     = 6
LR           = 3e-4
WARMUP_STEPS = 100
TOTAL_STEPS  = 1_000
GRAD_CLIP    = 1.0
DEVICE       = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"

# ----- Toy transformer block -----
class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.qkv  = nn.Linear(HIDDEN_DIM, 3 * HIDDEN_DIM)
        self.proj = nn.Linear(HIDDEN_DIM, HIDDEN_DIM)
        self.n_heads = N_HEADS
        self.head_dim = HIDDEN_DIM // N_HEADS

    def forward(self, x):
        B, T, C = x.shape
        q, k, v = self.qkv(x).split(HIDDEN_DIM, dim=-1)
        q = q.view(B, T, self.n_heads, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        k = k.view(B, T, self.n_heads, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        v = v.view(B, T, self.n_heads, self.head_dim).transpose(1, 2)
        # Causal mask — attend only to past tokens
        att = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / (self.head_dim ** 0.5)
        mask = torch.tril(torch.ones(T, T, device=x.device)).bool()
        att = att.masked_fill(~mask, float("-inf"))
        att = torch.softmax(att, dim=-1)
        out = (att @ v).transpose(1, 2).reshape(B, T, C)
        return self.proj(out)

class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention()
        self.ff   = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(HIDDEN_DIM, 4 * HIDDEN_DIM), nn.GELU(),
            nn.Linear(4 * HIDDEN_DIM, HIDDEN_DIM)
        )
        self.ln1 = nn.LayerNorm(HIDDEN_DIM)
        self.ln2 = nn.LayerNorm(HIDDEN_DIM)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.ln1(x))
        x = x + self.ff(self.ln2(x))
        return x

class MiniGPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__()
        self.tok_emb = nn.Embedding(VOCAB_SIZE, HIDDEN_DIM)
        self.pos_emb = nn.Embedding(SEQ_LEN, HIDDEN_DIM)
        self.blocks  = nn.Sequential(*[Block() for _ in range(N_LAYERS)])
        self.ln_f    = nn.LayerNorm(HIDDEN_DIM)
        self.head    = nn.Linear(HIDDEN_DIM, VOCAB_SIZE, bias=False)

    def forward(self, idx):
        B, T = idx.shape
        pos  = torch.arange(T, device=idx.device)
        x    = self.tok_emb(idx) + self.pos_emb(pos)
        x    = self.blocks(x)
        x    = self.ln_f(x)
        return self.head(x)   # (B, T, vocab)

# ----- Training -----
model     = MiniGPT().to(DEVICE)
optimizer = AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=LR, betas=(0.9, 0.95), weight_decay=0.1)
scheduler = CosineAnnealingLR(optimizer, T_max=TOTAL_STEPS)
loss_fn   = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()

def linear_warmup(step, warmup=WARMUP_STEPS):
    return min(1.0, step / max(1, warmup))

for step in range(1, TOTAL_STEPS + 1):
    # Simulate random token batch (replace with real DataLoader in practice)
    tokens = torch.randint(0, VOCAB_SIZE, (BATCH_SIZE, SEQ_LEN + 1), device=DEVICE)
    x, y   = tokens[:, :-1], tokens[:, 1:]          # CLM: predict next token

    logits = model(x)                                 # (B, T, V)
    loss   = loss_fn(logits.reshape(-1, VOCAB_SIZE), y.reshape(-1))

    # Warmup override
    for pg in optimizer.param_groups:
        pg["lr"] = LR * linear_warmup(step)

    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), GRAD_CLIP)
    optimizer.step()
    scheduler.step()

    if step % 100 == 0:
        print(f"step {step:5d}  loss {loss.item():.4f}  lr {optimizer.param_groups[0]['lr']:.2e}")
Before training, raw text must be quality-filtered, deduplicated, tokenized, and packed into fixed-length sequences. This pipeline demonstrates each stage:

# Data preprocessing pipeline for LLM pretraining
# Demonstrates quality filtering, deduplication, and sequence packing

import hashlib
import random
from typing import Iterator

# ----- Quality filter -----
def passes_quality_filter(text: str, min_words: int = 20) -> bool:
    """Basic heuristics used in practice (simplified)."""
    words = text.split()
    if len(words) < min_words:
        return False
    # Reject if >30 % tokens are symbols / numbers
    alpha_ratio = sum(c.isalpha() for c in text) / max(1, len(text))
    if alpha_ratio < 0.70:
        return False
    # Reject if duplicate lines make up >20 % of content
    lines = text.splitlines()
    unique = set(lines)
    if len(unique) / max(1, len(lines)) < 0.80:
        return False
    return True

# ----- Exact-match deduplication (MinHash is used at scale) -----
class ExactDeduplicator:
    def __init__(self):
        self._seen: set[str] = set()

    def is_duplicate(self, text: str) -> bool:
        digest = hashlib.sha256(text.strip().encode()).hexdigest()
        if digest in self._seen:
            return True
        self._seen.add(digest)
        return False

# ----- Tokenizer stub -----
def tokenize(text: str) -> list[int]:
    """Returns token IDs (stub — use tiktoken / SentencePiece in production)."""
    return [ord(c) % 1000 for c in text]   # dummy mapping

# ----- Sequence packing -----
def pack_sequences(
    documents: Iterator[list[int]],
    seq_len: int = 1024,
    eos_id: int = 2,
) -> Iterator[list[int]]:
    """
    Concatenate tokenized docs with EOS separator; yield non-overlapping
    fixed-length chunks.  Avoids per-doc padding waste.
    """
    buffer: list[int] = []
    for tokens in documents:
        buffer.extend(tokens)
        buffer.append(eos_id)
        while len(buffer) >= seq_len:
            yield buffer[:seq_len]
            buffer = buffer[seq_len:]

# ----- Full pipeline -----
def preprocess_corpus(raw_texts: list[str], seq_len: int = 1024):
    dedup = ExactDeduplicator()
    filtered = (
        t for t in raw_texts
        if passes_quality_filter(t) and not dedup.is_duplicate(t)
    )
    tokenized = (tokenize(t) for t in filtered)
    packed    = pack_sequences(tokenized, seq_len=seq_len)
    return list(packed)

# ----- Demo -----
sample_corpus = [
    "Large language models learn from massive text corpora without labels. " * 5,
    "Low quality!!!1!!",                             # filtered: low alpha ratio
    "Large language models learn from massive text corpora without labels. " * 5,  # duplicate
    "The transformer architecture uses self-attention to model dependencies "
    "between all positions in a sequence simultaneously. " * 4,
]
batches = preprocess_corpus(sample_corpus, seq_len=50)
print(f"Produced {len(batches)} packed sequences from {len(sample_corpus)} raw documents")
for i, batch in enumerate(batches[:3]):
    print(f"  batch {i}: {len(batch)} tokens  first={batch[:5]}...")
The most practical path to domain adaptation is restarting training from an existing checkpoint with a lower learning rate to avoid catastrophic forgetting of general capabilities:

# Continued pretraining (domain adaptation) on a pretrained HuggingFace model
# This is the approach used when you own a base model and want to specialize it.

from transformers import (
    AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM,
    DataCollatorForLanguageModeling, Trainer, TrainingArguments,
)
from datasets import Dataset
import torch

# ----- Load pretrained base checkpoint -----
MODEL_ID = "gpt2"   # stand-in; replace with llama-3-8b or similar
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(MODEL_ID)
tokenizer.pad_token = tokenizer.eos_token   # GPT-2 has no pad token
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    MODEL_ID,
    torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
    device_map="auto",
)

# ----- Domain-specific corpus -----
domain_texts = [
    "Clinical note: patient presents with acute myocardial infarction. " * 20,
    "ECG interpretation: sinus tachycardia with ST-segment elevation. " * 20,
    # ... thousands of documents in practice
]

def tokenize_fn(examples):
    return tokenizer(
        examples["text"],
        truncation=True,
        max_length=512,
        padding=False,          # packing collator handles this
        return_special_tokens_mask=True,
    )

raw_ds = Dataset.from_dict({"text": domain_texts})
tokenized_ds = raw_ds.map(tokenize_fn, batched=True, remove_columns=["text"])

# ----- Causal LM collator (masks padding in loss automatically) -----
collator = DataCollatorForLanguageModeling(tokenizer=tokenizer, mlm=False)

# ----- Training arguments for continued pretraining -----
args = TrainingArguments(
    output_dir="./domain-adapted-model",
    per_device_train_batch_size=4,
    gradient_accumulation_steps=8,       # effective batch = 32
    num_train_epochs=1,
    learning_rate=1e-5,                  # lower LR — avoid catastrophic forgetting
    lr_scheduler_type="cosine",
    warmup_ratio=0.05,
    bf16=True,
    logging_steps=10,
    save_steps=200,
    # Gradient checkpointing saves VRAM at the cost of 30 % more compute
    gradient_checkpointing=True,
)

trainer = Trainer(
    model=model,
    args=args,
    train_dataset=tokenized_ds,
    data_collator=collator,
)

# Uncomment to train (requires GPU):
# trainer.train()
# trainer.save_model("./domain-adapted-model/final")
print("Trainer configured.  Call trainer.train() to start continued pretraining.")
print(f"Model parameters: {sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters()) / 1e6:.1f}M")

Meta's Llama 3 family (2024) illustrates the key engineering decisions made at production scale. The 8 B and 70 B base models were pretrained on approximately 15 T tokens of curated text, which is roughly 3.5 × more than the Chinchilla-optimal quantity for these model sizes. This deliberate overtraining makes the models stronger at inference time even though the per-step loss improvement diminishes past the Chinchilla point—a practical trade-off when inference costs are high relative to training costs.

The training corpus was curated from web text, technical documents, scientific papers, and code, with special attention to boosting the fraction of code and multilingual content compared to earlier runs. The tokenizer uses a 128 k vocabulary BPE, roughly 4× larger than GPT-2's 50 k vocabulary, which improves tokenization efficiency for code, non-English scripts, and numeric content. Training used 16 k GPU clusters with tensor + pipeline + data parallelism. Gradient checkpointing allowed training the 70 B model in BF16 without running out of VRAM on A100 nodes.

The resulting base checkpoints were then instruction-tuned on a mixture of public and synthetic chat data and further aligned with DPO. For agentic deployments, teams typically start from the 8 B or 70 B instruct checkpoint and apply task-specific fine-tuning or retrieval augmentation on top—never returning to the base model.
Treating base and instruct checkpoints interchangeably.

Base models do not follow instructions reliably. A system prompt telling a base model to act as a JSON-only API will be ignored or inconsistently followed. Always use an instruct checkpoint as the backbone for agent systems.
Knowledge cutoffs are hard boundaries. A model trained through mid-2024 does not know about events after that date. Retrieval augmentation or external tools are required for up-to-date information.
Underestimating the impact of corpus mix.

A model pretrained primarily on English web text will underperform on French legal documents, Python code idioms in a niche library, or medical terminology—even after general instruction tuning. Continued pretraining on domain data is the fix, not prompt engineering.
Using too high a learning rate for continued pretraining.
Restarting at the same peak LR used in the original run causes catastrophic forgetting: the model rapidly overwrites general capabilities. Use a 5–10× lower LR (e.g., 1e-5 instead of 3e-4).

Simply padding every document to the max sequence length wastes tokens and GPU cycles. Packing multiple short documents into a single sequence with EOS separators fills the context window and improves GPU utilization significantly.

Duplicate documents in the training corpus cause the model to memorize those documents rather than learn from them. Near-duplicate deduplication with MinHash-LSH is standard practice and should not be skipped.

Perplexity is tokenizer-specific; comparing perplexity between a model with 32 k vocabulary and one with 128 k vocabulary is meaningless without normalization. Use bits-per-character (BPC) for cross-model comparisons.
Autoregressive generation is the standard interface for all modern agent-facing LLMs. Unless building a specialized retrieval encoder, CLM base models are the correct starting point.

Sample thousands of documents from the cleaned corpus and manually review them. Pervasive formatting artifacts, boilerplate repetition, or low-quality content caught early saves days of wasted training.

BF16 has the same memory footprint as FP16 but a much larger dynamic range, making loss spikes from gradient overflow far less common. Almost all modern training frameworks default to BF16 on A100/H100 hardware.
Monitor training loss and learning rate together.
A learning rate that is too high shows as loss spikes followed by recovery. A learning rate that decays too early shows as a plateau before the expected final loss. Log both metrics every step.
Hardware failures in long pretraining runs are inevitable. Save a full checkpoint every 500–1000 steps and retain multiple recent checkpoints in case a checkpoint is corrupted.
Clip at gradient norm 1.0. Without clipping, a single outlier batch can produce a gradient update that destabilizes training, causing a loss spike that takes hundreds of steps to recover from.
Scale batch size, not learning rate alone.

Increasing the global batch size (via gradient accumulation or more GPUs) often stabilizes training and improves final perplexity. Follow the linear scaling rule: double the LR when doubling the batch size.

Perplexity on held-out data is a good training signal but is not perfectly correlated with downstream agent performance. Run lightweight task benchmarks (code completion, instruction following) at intervals to catch regressions.
(Hoffmann et al., 2022): the optimal allocation of a fixed compute budget

This implies roughly 20 training tokens per parameter for an optimal run. At 7 B parameters, the Chinchilla-optimal token count is about 140 B tokens. Llama 3-8B used 15 T tokens—more than 100× the Chinchilla optimum—because inference cost per token dominates for widely-deployed models: a stronger model at the same parameter count is worth the extra training compute.

# Chinchilla scaling law calculator
# Estimates optimal tokens given a compute budget (FLOPs)

# Chinchilla (Hoffmann et al. 2022) optimal allocation:
#   N_opt ~ 0.1746 * C^0.5   (parameters)
#   D_opt ~ 3.826  * C^0.5   (training tokens)
# where C = total FLOPs.
# Rule of thumb: ~20 training tokens per parameter.

import math

def chinchilla_optimal(compute_flops: float) -> dict:
    """
    Given a compute budget in FLOPs, return the Chinchilla-optimal
    model size (parameters) and dataset size (tokens).
    """
    n_opt = 0.1746 * math.sqrt(compute_flops)
    d_opt = 3.826  * math.sqrt(compute_flops)
    return {"params": n_opt, "tokens": d_opt}

def estimate_compute(params: float, tokens: float) -> float:
    """
    Kaplan et al. approximation: C ≈ 6 * N * D
    (6 FLOPs per token per parameter: 2 forward + 2 backward + 2 weight update)
    """
    return 6 * params * tokens

# Example: H100 cluster, 1000 GPUs, 7 days
H100_FLOPS_PER_SEC = 2e15          # ~2 petaFLOP/s (bf16 MFU ~50 %)
GPUS               = 1_000
HOURS              = 7 * 24
compute_budget     = H100_FLOPS_PER_SEC * GPUS * HOURS * 3600

result = chinchilla_optimal(compute_budget)
print(f"Compute budget : {compute_budget:.2e} FLOPs")
print(f"Optimal params : {result['params'] / 1e9:.1f}B parameters")
print(f"Optimal tokens : {result['tokens'] / 1e12:.1f}T training tokens")

# Cross-check
c_est = estimate_compute(result["params"], result["tokens"])
print(f"Verify C ≈ 6ND : {c_est:.2e} FLOPs (should match budget)")

# Practical model sizes
for params_b in [7, 13, 34, 70]:
    params = params_b * 1e9
    tokens_for_budget = compute_budget / (6 * params)
    print(f"{params_b:3d}B model → {tokens_for_budget/1e12:.2f}T tokens with this budget")

—the fraction of theoretical peak FLOPs actually spent on useful matrix multiplications. A well-tuned run achieves 40–60 % MFU on H100s. Flash attention, fused kernels, and optimized communication (NVLink, InfiniBand) push MFU higher. The compute formula is approximately
FLOPs for a dense transformer (the factor of 6 accounts for forward pass, backward pass, and the parameter update).
Several open-source frameworks have become standard for large-scale pretraining:
(NVIDIA) — highly optimized tensor + pipeline + data parallelism for GPU clusters. Used for GPT-3-scale runs. Achieves near-peak MFU on A100/H100 hardware.

(Microsoft) — ZeRO optimizer partitions optimizer states, gradients, and parameters across GPUs, enabling models that do not fit in aggregate GPU memory. ZeRO-3 is the default for large continued-pretraining jobs.
— high-level API for training and continued pretraining. Accelerate handles distributed setup transparently. Best suited for teams that need flexibility rather than maximum throughput.
— minimal, readable implementations ideal for learning and experimentation. NanoGPT trains a small GPT-2 on a single GPU in hours.
— PyTorch-native large-scale training framework from Meta, using torch.distributed and FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel). Replaces custom Megatron integrations for teams on pure PyTorch stacks.
(Google) — reference tokenizer implementations. tiktoken is used by GPT-4 and Llama 3; SentencePiece by T5, mT5, and many multilingual models.
— open pretraining datasets. FineWeb (HuggingFace, 2024) is a high-quality English web corpus; Dolma provides multi-domain mixing; RedPajama reproduces the Llama data mix.
The table below compares the three main pretraining objectives across key dimensions relevant to agent and generation tasks.
Best — all chat LLMs use CLM
Each token produces one loss signal
Pretraining introduces security risks that propagate to all downstream products built on a checkpoint:

— an adversary who can inject content into the training corpus can influence model behavior at inference time. This is particularly concerning for models trained on live web crawls where the attacker can publish content before the crawl date. Mitigations include aggressive quality filtering, domain whitelisting, and adversarial content detection.

— models memorize training data, especially for high-frequency or near-duplicate documents. PII, API keys, credentials, or confidential documents present in the corpus can be extracted by an attacker via targeted prompting. Deduplication and PII scrubbing before training are essential.

— a poisoned corpus can cause a model to produce malicious outputs when a specific trigger phrase is present in the prompt. This is an active research area; defenses include outlier detection on training data and behavioral red-teaming.

— loss spikes from numerical overflow, corrupted data batches, or hardware failures can require rolling back to an earlier checkpoint. Frequent checkpointing (every 500–1000 steps), loss spike monitoring, and gradient norm logging are standard mitigations.

— applications must document the base model's training cutoff and design retrieval or tool-use patterns to compensate for stale knowledge. Undisclosed cutoffs cause user trust failures when the model confidently states outdated facts.

Think of pretraining as building a vast library inside the model's weights. Each gradient step is like the librarian reading one more page of text and updating an internal index of how language, facts, and concepts connect. After trillions of pages, the librarian has internalized a compressed representation of what was in the library—but only what was in the library.

Instruction tuning is like hiring a reference desk staff: they use the same library but have been trained to answer questions in a helpful, structured way rather than just reciting what they read. RLHF is like adding a supervisor who rates the answers and trains the staff to give better ones. Neither stage adds new books to the library; they only change how the library is accessed and what the staff decides to say.

This analogy makes several things concrete: you cannot ask the library about a book published after it was built (knowledge cutoff). You cannot ask a single-language library about another language it never ingested (language coverage). And you cannot fix a library built on misinformation just by retraining the reference desk—the wrong books are still there.
Pretraining establishes the capability ceiling of a base model; instruction tuning, RLHF, and prompting can only refine—never exceed—what pretraining built in.
Causal language modeling (CLM) is the dominant pretraining objective for all modern agent-facing LLMs; it trains models to predict the next token autoregressively.
The loss is the average negative log-likelihood summed over every token position, giving
supervision signals per sequence, which is why the objective scales efficiently.
Data quality—filtering, deduplication, and domain mix ratios—has as large an impact on final model quality as model size or total compute.
Continued pretraining at a lower learning rate (1e-5 vs 3e-4) is the practical path to domain adaptation for teams that cannot afford a full pretraining run.
Chinchilla scaling laws show that optimal compute allocation assigns roughly 20 training tokens per parameter; overtraining beyond this is justified when inference cost at scale is high.
Distributed training (data + tensor + pipeline parallelism, ZeRO, mixed precision) is mandatory for models beyond a few billion parameters.
Security risks introduced at pretraining—data poisoning, PII memorization, knowledge cutoffs—propagate to all downstream products and must be managed in the data pipeline, not the deployment layer.
For agent product development, the most important pretraining decisions to inspect are: training cutoff, domain coverage, code fraction in the corpus, and context window length.
